en.extract:
Bates's sunbird (Cinnyris batesi) is a species of sunbird in the family Nectariniidae which occurs in Western African forests and Central African rainforests, and locally in other types of forest in Central Africa.


Taxonomy
Monotypic.


Description
Bates's sunbird is a very small dull green sunbird with a short, decurved bill a short tail and a yellowish belly. The male has dark olive upperparts, except for a vague pale supercilium, with paler greyer underparts with a pale buffy centre to the belly. The wings are dark brown with pale fringes and the tail is black with wide olive edges and tips to the outer tail feathers. There are long wispy yellow feathers on the sides of the breast, in the otherwise similar female these feathers are whiter and may be much reduced in length. They measure 9–10 cm in length and weigh 5.7–7g. Given the species overall drab plumage and furtive habits, it can be easily overlooked.


Distribution
Bates's sunbird is an uncommon resident of forest and rainforest from Liberia in the west to the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo in the east, occurring south as far as Angola and northern Zambia. This species is also known from Bioko.


Habitat
Bates's sunbird is found in the canopy of trees in primary rainforest, also at forest edges, secondary growth and cultivated areas with tall trees.


Habits
Bates's sunbirds feed on nectar from blossoms on flowering trees, Macaranga assas is known to be fed on. Also insects, spiders, berries and fruits. It will form small flocks of ten or so and will also join mixed species flocks. They are often seen on branches with a lot of moss and epiphytes. At night it is able to reduce its metabolism to save energy.
The nest is a hanging pocket made of pappus, dry leaves and spiders webs with green moss placed on the outside and lined with natural cotton-like fibres, it is suspended from a bush at about 3m above the ground or even from cables in abandoned buildings. 1-2 eggs are laid in July in Cameroon, December in the Democratic Republic of Congo and February in Gabon.


Naming
Its common name and Latin binomial commemorate George Latimer Bates (1863–1940), an American naturalist who traveled in West Africa.


References

'''Bates's sunbird''' ('''''Cinnyris batesi''''') is a species of [[sunbird]] in the family [[Nectariniidae]] which occurs in [[Upper Guinean forests|Western African forests]] and [[Congolian rainforests|Central African rainforests]], and locally in other types of forest in Central Africa.

==Taxonomy==
Monotypic.<ref name="bow" />

==Description==
Bates's sunbird is a very small dull green sunbird with a short, decurved bill a short tail and a yellowish belly. The male has dark olive upperparts, except for a vague pale supercilium, with paler greyer underparts with a pale buffy centre to the belly. The wings are dark brown with pale fringes and the tail is black with wide olive edges and tips to the outer tail feathers. There are long wispy yellow feathers on the sides of the breast, in the otherwise similar female these feathers are whiter and may be much reduced in length.<ref name = Sunbirds>{{cite book | author1 = Robert A. Cheke | author2 = Clive F. Mann | author3 = Richard Allen | year =2001 | title = Sunbirds | publisher = Pica Press | pages = 312–313 |isbn = 1-873403-80-1}}</ref> They measure 9–10 cm in length and weigh 5.7–7g.<ref name = HBW>{{cite web | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/batess-sunbird-cinnyris-batesi | title = Bates's Sunbird (''Cinnyris batesi'') | access-date = 2016-11-12 | publisher = Lynx Edicions|work = HBW Alive}}</ref> Given the species overall drab plumage and furtive habits, it can be easily overlooked.<ref name="bow" />

==Distribution==
Bates's sunbird is an uncommon resident of forest and rainforest from [[Liberia]] in the west to the eastern [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]] in the east, occurring south as far as [[Angola]] and northern [[Zambia]].<ref name="Sunbirds" /><ref name="bow">{{cite web |last1=Cheke |first1=Robert |last2=Mann |first2=Clive |title=Bates's Sunbird (Cinnyris batesi), version 1.0 |website=Birds of the World | url = https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/batsun2/1.0/introduction |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |location=Ithaca, New York, USA |doi=10.2173/bow.batsun2.01 |date=4 March 2020}}</ref> This species is also known from [[Bioko]].<ref name="perez_del_val">{{cite book |last1=Pérez del Val |first1=Jaime |title=Las aves de Bioko, Guinea Ecuatorial: guía de campo |date=1996 |publisher=Edilesa |location=León, España |isbn=84-8012-145-9}}</ref>

==Habitat==
Bates's sunbird is found in the canopy of trees in primary rainforest, also at forest edges, secondary growth and cultivated areas with tall trees.<ref name = Sunbirds/>

==Habits==
Bates's sunbirds feed on nectar from blossoms on flowering trees, ''[[Macaranga assas]]'' is known to be fed on. Also insects, spiders, berries and fruits. It will form small flocks of ten or so and will also join mixed species flocks. They are often seen on branches with a lot of moss and [[epiphyte]]s. At night it is able to reduce its metabolism to save energy.<ref name = Sunbirds/>

The nest is a hanging pocket made of [[Pappus (flower structure)|pappus]], dry leaves and spiders webs with green moss placed on the outside and lined with natural cotton-like fibres, it is suspended from a bush at about 3m above the ground or even from cables in abandoned buildings. 1-2 eggs are laid in July in Cameroon, December in the Democratic Republic of Congo and February in Gabon.<ref name = Sunbirds/>

==Naming==
Its common name and Latin binomial commemorate [[George Latimer Bates]] (1863–1940), an American naturalist who traveled in West Africa.<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=42|author2=Michael Watkins}}</ref>
